title: Lecture 2
subtitle: Scrum Framework
description: 65 deep Q&A questions on Scrum principles, aspects, personas, user stories, backlogs, and velocity.

questions.q: What does Empirical Process Control mean in Scrum?
opts: Progress is driven by observation and experimentation, not rigid upfront plans
Managers review and control all team decisions at each stage
Processes are audited and certified by an external body
All work is fully defined and planned before the sprint starts
questions.explain: Empirical = observation + experimentation. The team inspects what actually happened each sprint and adapts — rather than following a pre-written script.
questions.deep: Every Sprint Review and Retrospective is an act of Empirical Process Control. You observe reality, then adjust your plan based on what you found — not what you assumed.

questions.q: The Self-Organization principle means tasks in a sprint are assigned by whom?
opts: The Product Owner assigns tasks by business priority
The Development Team collectively decides who does what
A project manager allocates work to each developer
The Scrum Master assigns tasks based on skills
questions.explain: Self-organization means the Development Team chooses how to accomplish its own work. No one — not even the Scrum Master — assigns tasks.
questions.deep: Self-organising teams consistently outperform directed ones. People who choose their own work take ownership, solve problems more creatively, and produce higher-quality output.

questions.q: What does Time-boxing mean when applied to Scrum events?
opts: Daily Standups have a suggested but flexible time limit
Sprints can be extended if the team needs a few more days
Every Scrum event has a fixed maximum duration that cannot be extended
Estimating all work in hours before the sprint begins
questions.explain: Time-boxing means every Scrum event has a fixed maximum duration. The Daily Standup is 15 minutes, a Sprint is 1–4 weeks — hard limits, not suggestions.
questions.deep: Time-boxing creates discipline and focus. When meetings have hard end times, teams stay on topic and avoid the "meeting creep" that plagues traditional organisations.

questions.q: How long is the Daily Scrum, and does its length change with sprint length?
opts: It is however long the team needs to discuss all blockers
It is 1 hour for a 4-week sprint and 15 minutes for a 1-week sprint
It is 30 minutes and scales up for longer sprints
It is 15 minutes regardless of sprint length
questions.explain: The Daily Scrum is always 15 minutes — it does not scale with sprint length. The fixed duration keeps the event efficient.
questions.deep: The 15-minute limit forces brevity. Complex technical issues should be discussed in a follow-up meeting — never during the Standup itself.

questions.q: For a 2-week sprint, how long is Sprint Planning time-boxed to?
opts: 4 hours — two hours per week of the sprint
1 hour — enough to agree on the sprint goal
8 hours — a full day for thorough planning
2 hours — one hour per week of the sprint
questions.explain: Sprint Planning = 2 hours per week of sprint length. A 2-week sprint = 4 hours of Sprint Planning.
questions.deep: The formula scales: a 4-week sprint = 8 hours of planning. This ensures enough time to commit confidently without spending the sprint planning it.

questions.q: What is the key difference between Empirical and Defined Process Control?
opts: Empirical suits small teams; defined suits large enterprise teams
Empirical adapts based on observation; defined assumes all work is understood upfront
Empirical is the approach used in the Waterfall model
Empirical requires more documentation to track findings
questions.explain: Defined process assumes full upfront understanding. Empirical process expects the unexpected and builds in regular inspect-and-adapt cycles.
questions.deep: Software is inherently complex and unpredictable — which is why Empirical Process Control fits. Waterfall's defined approach fails because it assumes certainty that rarely exists.

questions.q: Value-based Prioritization means the team builds the backlog in which order?
opts: Most technically complex stories first to reduce risk early
Stories in the order the client originally listed them
Highest business value stories first to maximise ROI from the earliest sprints
Shortest stories first to build team momentum and velocity
questions.explain: Value-based Prioritization = highest business value stories are built first. The Product Owner determines business value — not the development team.
questions.deep: This ensures maximum ROI from the earliest sprints. Even if a project is cancelled after Sprint 1, the most important features have already been delivered.

questions.q: What does the Iterative Development principle mean for how user stories are delivered?
opts: All stories are bundled into a single release at the end of the project
Stories are randomly selected each sprint based on team preference
One story is selected and fully completed before any other work begins
Stories are implemented gradually across multiple sprints, each sprint building on the last
questions.explain: Iterative Development means stories are delivered gradually across multiple sprints — each iteration improves and builds on what came before.
questions.deep: This is why Scrum produces working software every sprint rather than one large release. Continuous delivery means continuous value and continuous user feedback.

questions.q: A team routinely extends Daily Standups to 45 minutes to resolve technical issues. Which Scrum principle is most directly violated?
opts: Time-boxing — the Standup has a fixed 15-minute maximum that is being ignored
Collaboration — the extended meetings reduce time for actual collaboration
Self-organization — the team should be directing themselves without outside input
Value-based Prioritization — the discussion is not focused on backlog value
questions.explain: Time-boxing is violated. The Daily Standup must be 15 minutes maximum. Technical problem-solving belongs in a separate follow-up meeting, not the Standup.
questions.deep: Allowing Standup overruns sets a precedent that destroys the event's value. The Scrum Master should end the meeting at 15 minutes and schedule a follow-up for deep technical discussion.

questions.q: The Agile Manifesto values "customer collaboration over contract negotiation." Which Scrum principle most directly puts this into practice?
opts: Empirical Process Control — adapting based on feedback reduces reliance on contracts
Collaboration — continuous teamwork with the client replaces adversarial contract-based relationships
Time-boxing — fixed sprint durations replace open-ended contractual milestones
Iterative Development — delivering incrementally reduces the need for upfront contracts
questions.explain: The Collaboration principle most directly embodies this — continuous collaboration between the Scrum team and the client, replacing the need to hide behind legal contracts.
questions.deep: The Sprint Review is the primary collaboration checkpoint. Clients see real working software every sprint and shape what comes next — making rigid upfront contracts less necessary.

questions.q: Why does Scrum generally prefer shorter sprints (1–2 weeks) over longer ones (4–6 weeks)?
opts: Shorter sprints reduce the number of story points the team needs to commit to
Shorter sprints cost less because ceremonies take up proportionally less time
Shorter sprints create more frequent inspect-and-adapt cycles, surfacing problems faster
Longer sprints require more complex ceremonies that slow the team down
questions.explain: Shorter sprints = faster feedback loops. Problems are discovered in 1–2 weeks instead of 4–6 weeks, making them far cheaper to fix.
questions.deep: A 2-week sprint gives 26 opportunities to inspect and adapt per year. A 6-week sprint gives only 8. More frequent feedback = faster learning, faster course correction.

questions.q: Co-location is highlighted under the Collaboration principle. Why is it specifically mentioned?
opts: It is required for every Scrum event to be valid
It lowers office costs and reduces the project budget
It lets Scrum Masters watch team behaviour closely
It gives teams richer communication and faster feedback
questions.explain: Face-to-face communication is richer than any digital alternative. Co-located teams collaborate more naturally and more frequently.
questions.deep: Face-to-face work reduces delay and misunderstanding. Remote teams compensate with video calls, shared boards, and real-time chat tools.

questions.q: How many Scrum Aspects are there, and what do they represent?
opts: There are 5 — they are ongoing dimensions that must be managed throughout every Scrum project
There are 4 — they map directly to the 4 Agile Manifesto values
There are 3 — they are the core roles of the Scrum team
There are 6 — they mirror the 6 Scrum Principles exactly
questions.explain: 5 Scrum Aspects: Organization, Business Justification, Quality, Change, and Risk. They are ongoing concerns — not one-time steps.
questions.deep: Unlike Processes (sequential steps), Aspects must be actively managed throughout the entire project lifecycle. They never stop requiring attention.

questions.q: Which Scrum Aspect defines the difference between core and non-core roles?
opts: Business Justification — it defines who has financial responsibility
Organization — it defines how the Scrum team is structured and who is accountable
Risk — it defines which team members manage project risks
Quality — it defines who is responsible for meeting acceptance criteria
questions.explain: The Organization aspect defines team structure — including core roles (Scrum Master, Product Owner, Dev Team) and non-core roles (stakeholders, vendors, Scrum Guidance Body).
questions.deep: Knowing which roles are core vs non-core clarifies accountability. Core roles are committed to sprint delivery; non-core are involved but not accountable.

questions.q: What is the key distinction between Core and Non-Core Scrum roles?
opts: Core roles are paid; non-core roles are usually external
Core roles write code; non-core roles provide requirements
Core roles are accountable; non-core roles provide input
Core roles attend all events; non-core roles attend reviews
questions.explain: Core roles (SM, PO, Dev Team) are committed to and accountable for sprint delivery. Non-core roles (stakeholders, vendors) are involved but not accountable.
questions.deep: This distinction matters when things go wrong. If a sprint fails, core roles are responsible. Non-core roles provide input and feedback but do not bear accountability for the outcome.

questions.q: What is Backlog Grooming, and when does it happen?
opts: A one-time event at the start of a project to create the initial backlog
A monthly meeting where the PO assigns story points to upcoming items
A ceremony at the end of each sprint to remove completed stories
A continuous process of reviewing, refining, and re-prioritising backlog items as requirements evolve
questions.explain: Grooming = continuously reviewing, refining, re-prioritising, and updating backlog items throughout the project as requirements change.
questions.deep: Grooming is triggered by change. Regular grooming sessions keep the backlog relevant and accurate — ensuring the team always works on the most current, highest-value items.

questions.q: What is the difference between Acceptance Criteria and the Definition of Done?
opts: Acceptance Criteria are specific to one story; the Definition of Done applies to all stories in a sprint
They are interchangeable terms for the same concept in Scrum
The Definition of Done is optional; Acceptance Criteria are mandatory for every story
Acceptance Criteria are written by the dev team; the Definition of Done is written by stakeholders
questions.explain: Acceptance Criteria are per-story conditions (did this story do what it promised?). The Definition of Done is a universal checklist all stories must pass — code review, testing, documentation, etc.
questions.deep: A story can fully meet its Acceptance Criteria but still fail the Definition of Done (e.g., the code was never reviewed). Both must pass before a story is considered complete.

questions.q: Business Justification recognises two types of project value. What are they?
opts: Internal value (for the development team) and external value (for the client)
Tangible value (measurable with financial tools) and Intangible value (satisfaction, brand, social impact)
Quantitative value (numbers) and qualitative value (user feedback)
Short-term value (immediate revenue) and long-term value (future savings)
questions.explain: Tangible value = measurable (ROI, NPV, IRR). Intangible value = harder to quantify (customer satisfaction, brand reputation, public service).
questions.deep: Both matter equally. A charity app may have zero direct revenue but enormous social impact. Business Justification must consider both types to make sound project decisions.

questions.q: How does the Change aspect of Scrum differ from traditional project management's view of change?
opts: Scrum and traditional projects both view change as a necessary cost to manage carefully
Both approaches use formal change request processes but Scrum processes them faster
Scrum views change as an opportunity for improvement; traditional projects typically view it as a threat to control
Traditional projects welcome all change; Scrum restricts changes to between sprints only
questions.explain: Scrum views change as an opportunity — welcomed and managed through a formalised process. Traditional projects typically treat change as a risk to resist.
questions.deep: This mindset shift is fundamental. A client change request in Scrum is a chance to deliver more value. In Waterfall, it's a threat that could derail months of work.

questions.q: What role does the Scrum Guidance Body play within an organisation?
opts: It replaces the Product Owner's role in prioritising the backlog
It certifies developers and Scrum Masters within the organisation
It resolves conflicts between the Scrum Master and the development team
It creates guidelines and standards to keep multiple Scrum projects consistent across the organisation
questions.explain: The Scrum Guidance Body is an organisation-specific team that creates guidelines and documents to ensure Scrum projects remain consistent company-wide.
questions.deep: Large organisations running 50+ Scrum teams need shared coordination. The Guidance Body provides common quality standards, risk policies, and process guidelines so teams don't reinvent the wheel independently.

questions.q: A project has very high business value but also very high technical risk. How should Scrum prioritise this?
opts: High-risk stories should be implemented in early sprints to validate assumptions cheaply before investing further
Risk considerations should be ignored if the Product Owner confirms business value
High business value always overrides risk — build it first regardless of uncertainty
The Scrum Master has final say over whether to proceed with high-risk work
questions.explain: High-risk stories should be prioritised early. Discovering an impossibility or major technical obstacle in Sprint 1 is far cheaper than discovering it in Sprint 10.
questions.deep: This is the Technology Risk-Based prioritisation technique from Lecture 3. It counter-intuitively increases priority for risky stories. The goal is to fail fast and fail cheap.

questions.q: A story fully meets its Acceptance Criteria but the code was never reviewed by another team member. Is the story done?
opts: Yes — Acceptance Criteria and the Definition of Done are the same thing
No — the Definition of Done typically requires peer code review, and this story has not met that standard
Yes — if the Product Owner accepts it, it is considered complete
It depends on whether the Scrum Master has reviewed the code instead
questions.explain: No. The Definition of Done typically requires code review. Meeting Acceptance Criteria alone is not sufficient — both must be satisfied before a story is truly complete.
questions.deep: Skipping the Definition of Done creates hidden technical debt. The Product Owner should not accept a story until every item on the DoD checklist is confirmed complete.

questions.q: Of the 5 Risk Management steps, which one ensures that nobody on the project is surprised by a known threat?
opts: Risk Assessment — measuring the probability and impact of each risk
Risk Identification — finding risks before they materialise
Risk Communication — ensuring all stakeholders are informed of identified risks and mitigation plans
Risk Prioritization — ranking risks so the team tackles the most dangerous ones first
questions.explain: Risk Communication — the fifth step — ensures all stakeholders are informed about risks and what is being done about them. No one should be surprised.
questions.deep: Transparency is a core Scrum value. Hidden risks eventually become expensive surprises. When everyone knows what risks exist, they can contribute to mitigation and adjust expectations proactively.

questions.q: The Quality aspect warns that increasing scope without increasing time or resources reduces quality. What Scrum mechanism prevents this from happening mid-sprint?
opts: The Product Owner rejects all scope changes by default
The Scrum Master rejects any added work above a limit
Velocity automatically absorbs any added sprint work
Time-boxing keeps the sprint end fixed unless scope changes
questions.explain: Time-boxing is the primary defence. A sprint ends on its fixed date — adding stories without removing others means something will be rushed or incomplete.
questions.deep: This is why the Sprint Backlog is fixed once the sprint starts. Mid-sprint additions go into the Product Backlog for the next sprint — not into the current one.

questions.q: What is a user persona in the context of Scrum and UX design?
opts: A fictional archetype built from real user research, representing a distinct type of user
A real user recruited to test the product during User Acceptance Testing
A detailed description of the ideal technical skills for a developer on the team
A template that replaces user stories for non-technical stakeholders
questions.explain: A persona is a fictional archetype built from real user research — not invented from imagination. It represents a distinct type of user with specific goals and frustrations.
questions.deep: "Fictional" does not mean made up. Personas are composite characters grounded in data from real user observations, interviews, and surveys.

questions.q: What user research techniques are used to gather the data needed to create a persona?
opts: Reading competitor documentation and reviewing industry reports
Task analysis (card sorting, first-click testing), contextual interviews, focus groups, and prototyping
Asking developers to imagine what users would want based on their experience
Reviewing the product backlog and estimating user preferences from story titles
questions.explain: Persona data comes from: task analysis (card sorting, first-click testing), feedback methods (contextual interviews, focus groups), and prototyping with real users.
questions.deep: Each technique captures a different dimension. Interviews reveal motivations and frustrations. Card sorting reveals how users mentally categorise things. Good personas combine multiple techniques.

questions.q: What information is typically captured in a persona template?
opts: Technical architecture preferences, preferred programming languages, and hardware specs
Sprint velocity, story point estimates, and Definition of Done criteria
Name, demographics, role, goals, frustrations, motivations, behaviours, and personality traits
Project budget, delivery timeline, and risk assessment scores
questions.explain: A persona template captures: name, demographics, role, goals, frustrations, motivations, behaviour patterns, personality traits, and frequently used tools or apps.
questions.deep: Specificity is what makes personas useful. Generic details like "female, 40s, professional" are too vague to drive design decisions. Concrete details make the persona feel like a real person.

questions.q: Why must each persona in Scrum represent only a single user role, even if one real person has multiple roles?
opts: To keep persona documents short enough to read in one sitting
The Scrum Guide prohibits assigning more than one role per persona document
Having fewer personas speeds up the user story writing process
Mixing multiple roles in one persona creates conflicting requirements that make user stories contradictory
questions.explain: One person may hold multiple real-world roles, but mixing those roles into a single persona creates contradictory requirements. Each persona focuses on one role's specific goals and frustrations.
questions.deep: A person who is both a mobile banking customer and a small business owner has very different needs in each role. Separate personas — one for each role — keep requirements clean and unambiguous.

questions.q: What is the primary benefit of using personas when a development team writes user stories?
opts: Personas help the team empathise with real user needs rather than designing for an imaginary average user
Personas reduce the total number of user stories needed in the backlog
Personas automatically generate acceptance criteria for each story
Personas eliminate the need for separate user research during development
questions.explain: Personas help teams empathise with users — instead of designing for a vague "average user," the team designs for a specific person with known goals and frustrations.
questions.deep: Asking "would this persona be able to do this?" is far more actionable than "would a user be able to do this?" Concrete personas produce concrete, user-centred design decisions.

questions.q: How does a persona's stated goal or frustration directly influence the user stories written for them?
opts: The frustration becomes the acceptance criteria for a related story
The goal or frustration translates into the "feature" and "reason" parts of the user story format
The goal becomes the sprint goal for the team's next iteration
Goals and frustrations are used only for marketing materials, not story writing
questions.explain: A persona's goal becomes the "I want [feature]" part of the story, and their frustration informs the "because [reason]" part — directly shaping what gets built and why.
questions.deep: A persona who is frustrated by slow, paper-based loan applications and needs to manage their business on the go will produce stories centred on speed and mobile access — not desktop features.

questions.q: A team skips persona creation and jumps straight to writing user stories. What is the most significant risk?
opts: The backlog may be too short to fill the first sprint
Stories may use the wrong format without a template
Stories may reflect team assumptions instead of user needs
Story estimates may rise because context is missing
questions.explain: Without research-backed personas, user stories reflect developer assumptions — which frequently do not match reality. The result is often software users find confusing or irrelevant.
questions.deep: This is "building to assumptions" — one of the most common causes of product failure. Features that seemed obvious to developers often turn out to be unwanted or unused by actual users.

questions.q: A persona is described as someone who is time-poor, mobile-first, and needs to complete financial tasks quickly without visiting a branch. How should this persona influence story writing?
opts: Stories should prioritise complex desktop interfaces with detailed financial reporting tools
Stories should focus on improving branch booking and appointment scheduling features
Stories should capture all possible financial features regardless of complexity or time required
Stories should focus on fast, mobile-optimised workflows that minimise steps and avoid paperwork
questions.explain: A time-poor, mobile-first persona translates directly into stories that prioritise speed, simplicity, and mobile access — not complex desktop features or paper-based processes.
questions.deep: This is how personas prevent feature bloat. When the team asks "would a time-poor mobile user need this desktop report?" the answer guides prioritisation and prevents building for the wrong audience.

questions.q: What is the standard format for writing a user story?
opts: As a [role], I want [feature] because [reason]
Who: [user type], What: [feature needed], Why: [business reason]
Given [context], When [action], Then [expected outcome]
Feature: [name], Priority: [level], Points: [estimate]
questions.explain: "As a [role], I want [feature] because [reason]." This format keeps the focus on WHO benefits, WHAT they need, and WHY it matters.
questions.deep: The "because" part is often skipped but it's the most important. Stories without a clear reason often result in features that are technically delivered but practically useless.

questions.q: In the story hierarchy (Theme → Epic → Story → Task), what sits directly below a user story?
opts: Epic — a larger story that has not yet been broken down
Task — the technical implementation step needed to deliver the story
Theme — a grouping of related stories by business objective
Sprint — the time-boxed iteration that contains the story
questions.explain: Tasks sit directly below stories. They are the concrete technical steps needed to implement the story, estimated in hours rather than story points.
questions.deep: A single story might have 5–10 tasks covering different system layers — front-end, back-end, database, tests, documentation. Tasks are assigned during Sprint Planning.

questions.q: What is an Epic in the user story hierarchy?
opts: A group of stories that share a common business theme or objective
A technical task that requires specialist knowledge to complete
A large user story that is too big for one sprint and must be broken into smaller stories
A sprint that contains more stories than the team can complete
questions.explain: An Epic is a large user story — too big to complete in a single sprint — that must be decomposed into multiple smaller, sprint-sized stories.
questions.deep: "Build a complete e-commerce checkout" is an Epic. It breaks into: view cart, enter shipping, enter payment, confirm order — each a separate estimable story.

questions.q: What is a Theme in the user story hierarchy?
opts: The sprint goal agreed upon during Sprint Planning
The technical implementation task assigned to a specific developer
A large story that spans several sprints before it can be delivered
A group of user stories that share a common attribute or business goal
questions.explain: A Theme is a group of user stories sharing a common business goal or attribute. e.g. "All authentication-related stories" form a Theme.
questions.deep: Themes provide high-level roadmap visibility. A product might have Themes for Authentication, Payments, and Reporting — each containing multiple Epics and Stories underneath.

questions.q: Why are user stories generally preferred over traditional detailed requirements documents?
opts: They focus on user value rather than technical specification, making them easier to estimate and prioritise
They eliminate the need for acceptance testing once the story is delivered
They are written faster, which means less time is spent in planning meetings
They are legally binding and protect both the client and the development team
questions.explain: User stories focus on the holistic user value delivered — not on technical specifications. This makes them easier to estimate, prioritise, and test.
questions.deep: A 200-page spec describes features technically. A user story describes the same feature from the user's perspective. The story drives development toward value rather than toward specification compliance.

questions.q: What does "holistic" mean when describing the focus of user stories?
opts: Stories that relate to the entire product backlog rather than a single sprint
Stories that focus on the overall user experience and value delivered, not just one technical component
Stories that involve all team members in their design and estimation
Stories that are very large and cover multiple features across the product
questions.explain: "Holistic" means the story captures the complete user need and the value delivered — not just the technical feature to build.
questions.deep: "Build a search API endpoint" = technical artifact focus. "As a patron, I can search items to find books online" = holistic user value. The second produces better product decisions.

questions.q: How are tasks estimated differently from user stories, and why?
opts: Both use hours because Scrum requires all effort to be tracked in time units
Both use story points because they are both units of backlog work
Tasks use hours; stories use story points — two different levels of planning precision for different purposes
Tasks use story points; stories use hours — tasks are more complex and need precise sizing
questions.explain: Stories use story points (relative effort for high-level planning). Tasks use hours (actual time for sprint-level scheduling). Two levels of precision for two different purposes.
questions.deep: Points allow estimation without knowing exact implementation details. Hours require knowing exactly what you'll build and how. Using both avoids false precision at the wrong level.

questions.q: A user story must have a "role" in its format. Why is the role component so important?
opts: It is required for legal compliance with software delivery contracts
It determines the story point estimate — senior roles get higher estimates
It helps the Scrum Master assign the story to the right developer on the team
It connects the feature to a real user need, ensuring the story delivers value to a specific person
questions.explain: The role component connects the feature to a specific user's need. Without it, the story risks delivering a technically correct feature that serves nobody in particular.
questions.deep: A story with no clear role — like "As a system, the database should..." — loses the connection to user value entirely. The role is what makes user stories user-centred.

questions.q: A story is written as: "As a system, the database should be normalised to 3rd Normal Form." What is wrong with this story?
opts: The role is not a user, so it is really a task
The story needs points before entering the backlog
The story is an Epic because database work is large
The story is too long and should be shortened
questions.explain: "As a system" is not a valid user role. This is a technical task — it belongs in the task layer or as a technical backlog item, not as a user story.
questions.deep: This is the "technical story anti-pattern." Technical work must still be done, but framing it as a user story loses its connection to user value. Keep it as a task under a relevant story.

questions.q: A story reads: "As a citizen, I want to view a calendar, receive notifications, submit repairs, see contamination photos, and track complaints." What is wrong?
opts: The story is too short and needs more detail to be estimable
This is an Epic — it contains five distinct features that must each become their own separate story
The user role "citizen" is too broad and needs to be a specific persona
The story is correctly formatted and captures a complete user journey
questions.explain: This is an Epic, not a story. It contains five separate features — each needs its own story small enough to be completed within a single sprint.
questions.deep: The INVEST criteria: Independent, Negotiable, Valuable, Estimable, Small, Testable. This fails "Small." Breaking it up makes estimation, testing, and sprint planning far more manageable.

questions.q: How do user stories, story points, and velocity combine to give a team a release forecast?
opts: The Scrum Master manually calculates release date based on the complexity of each story
Story points and velocity are only used for internal planning and cannot produce a reliable release estimate
Total backlog story points divided by the team's average velocity gives the approximate number of sprints to complete the backlog
Stories are counted, multiplied by sprint length, and divided by team size to estimate release date
questions.explain: Total backlog points ÷ team velocity = approximate sprints to completion. If the backlog has 400 points and velocity is 40 pts/sprint, the release is roughly 10 sprints away.
questions.deep: Without small, estimable stories this calculation is impossible. The release forecast emerges bottom-up from real team data — not top-down from a manager's deadline.

questions.q: Who owns the Product Backlog and what does "ownership" mean in this context?
opts: The Development Team owns it and collectively decides the order of stories
Stakeholders own it and update it after each Sprint Review based on their feedback
The Scrum Master owns it and decides which stories the team will work on each sprint
The Product Owner owns it — meaning they decide the order and priority of all items in it
questions.explain: The Product Owner owns and prioritises the Product Backlog. Ownership means deciding the ORDER of stories — what gets built first.
questions.deep: The PO decides WHAT gets built and in what order. The Dev Team decides HOW to build it. This separation of concerns is fundamental to Scrum.

questions.q: Who owns the Sprint Backlog and why does ownership matter?
opts: The Development Team — they committed to these stories and are responsible for delivering them
The Scrum Master — they facilitate the sprint and are accountable for its outcome
The whole Scrum team jointly — all three roles share equal ownership
The Product Owner — they selected the stories during Sprint Planning
questions.explain: The Development Team owns the Sprint Backlog — they committed to these stories and are accountable for delivering them by sprint end.
questions.deep: Once the Sprint Backlog is set, only the Dev Team can modify it. This protects their commitment from mid-sprint disruption by stakeholders or the PO.

questions.q: What are the 4 types of items that can appear in a Product Backlog?
opts: Must have, Should have, Could have, and Won't have items
Features, Bugs, Technical work, and Knowledge acquisition
Requirements, designs, test plans, and documentation
User stories, epics, themes, and tasks
questions.explain: The 4 Product Backlog item types are: Features (user stories), Bugs (defects), Technical work (infrastructure), and Knowledge acquisition (research spikes).
questions.deep: Treating all four as first-class backlog items ensures they are visible, estimated, and prioritised — not hidden as "extra work" that silently erodes team velocity.

questions.q: Why is the Product Backlog described as "never finished"?
opts: Because the Scrum Master removes items from it faster than the PO can add new ones
Because it is a sign of poor planning that the team must address before the next sprint
Because the backlog is a living document that continuously grows as the product and market evolve
Because teams rarely complete all their backlog items, leaving them permanently incomplete
questions.explain: The Product Backlog is a living document — as the product evolves, new needs emerge, bugs surface, and market conditions change. It never stops growing.
questions.deep: A "finished" backlog would imply the team perfectly predicted all future needs upfront — which is impossible. An always-evolving backlog signals a healthy, learning product team.

questions.q: What is a knowledge acquisition backlog item, and when would a team add one?
opts: A bug task with an unknown root cause
A course for learning a new language
A story for onboarding new team members
A research spike that reduces uncertainty
questions.explain: A knowledge acquisition item is a time-boxed research spike. The output is a decision or recommendation — not code. Used to reduce uncertainty before committing to a solution.
questions.deep: A 2-day spike evaluating three frameworks can save months of expensive rework if the wrong framework would otherwise have been chosen. Spikes prevent costly irreversible decisions.

questions.q: What does a sprint burn-down chart track, and how is it used?
opts: It tracks remaining work against remaining sprint time
It tracks individual planned versus completed tasks
It tracks bugs found and fixed during the sprint
It tracks completed versus planned points across sprints
questions.explain: A burn-down chart shows work remaining (y-axis) vs time remaining (x-axis). If the actual line sits above the ideal diagonal, the team is behind schedule.
questions.deep: The burn-down chart is Empirical Process Control made visual — the team inspects it daily during the Standup and adapts their plan based on what it reveals.

questions.q: Who can make changes to the Sprint Backlog once the sprint has started?
opts: Stakeholders can request changes directly if they are classified as urgent
Only the Development Team can change the Sprint Backlog during a sprint
Only the Product Owner can change it, since they own the backlog
Anyone on the Scrum team, as long as the Scrum Master approves the change
questions.explain: Only the Development Team can modify the Sprint Backlog during a sprint. This protects their commitment from mid-sprint disruption.
questions.deep: Without this protection, stakeholders could constantly inject "urgent" items, making it impossible for the team to deliver on their commitments. The sprint is a protected container.

questions.q: Why are bugs added to the Product Backlog rather than fixed immediately when discovered?
opts: Because bugs are never urgent and always wait
Because every bug needs points before work starts
So bugs are visible and prioritised with other work
Because developers cannot fix unassigned bugs
questions.explain: Treating bugs as backlog items makes them visible, estimated, and rationally prioritised against other work. Not all bugs are equally important.
questions.deep: A critical data loss bug gets highest priority. A minor cosmetic issue may rank below valuable new features. Backlog treatment enables rational prioritisation instead of reactive fire-fighting.

questions.q: A Product Owner wants to add an urgent new story midway through the sprint. What is the correct Scrum response?
opts: Let the Scrum Master decide if it enters the sprint
Add it to the Sprint Backlog so work can start now
Reject it because sprint changes are never allowed
Add it to the Product Backlog for a future sprint
questions.explain: New stories should go to the Product Backlog for the next sprint. Only a genuine product-threatening emergency warrants cancelling the current sprint entirely.
questions.deep: Sprint protection is one of Scrum's most important mechanisms. If stakeholders can inject stories mid-sprint, the team can never commit to anything — and trust in the process collapses.

questions.q: On Day 7 of a 10-day sprint, only 4 of 10 stories are done and the burn-down shows the team is significantly behind. What should happen?
opts: Re-prioritise and focus on highest-value work
Cancel the sprint and restart with less work
Extend the sprint so all committed stories finish
Use overtime to finish every story on time
questions.explain: Re-prioritise within the sprint. Focus remaining capacity on the highest-value stories. Lower-priority stories can spill to the next sprint. The sprint ends on time.
questions.deep: This is Empirical Process Control applied: inspect the situation (4/10 done, 3 days left) and adapt (focus on what matters most). Extending or cancelling are extreme options for rare situations.

questions.q: Why is the Product Backlog described as "prioritised" rather than simply "ordered"?
opts: Prioritised means the Scrum Master ranks items
Prioritised means business value drives the order
The two terms mean exactly the same thing
Ordered means only technical dependencies matter
questions.explain: "Prioritised" means the order reflects deliberate business-value judgment — not arbitrary sequencing. The PO continuously reassesses whether the top items remain the most valuable.
questions.deep: As market conditions, user feedback, and business goals evolve, what was the highest-priority story last month may no longer be. Ongoing prioritisation keeps the backlog aligned with current reality.

questions.q: What is velocity in Scrum, and what does it measure?
opts: The average time taken to deliver a single user story from start to acceptance
The speed at which individual developers write and commit code each day
The number of story points a team completes per sprint — only fully done stories count
The total number of sprints completed since the project began
questions.explain: Velocity = the number of story points completed per sprint. Only stories that fully meet the Definition of Done count. Partially done stories contribute zero.
questions.deep: Velocity measures team throughput — how much complete, working software is delivered per sprint. Partial completion never counts, which enforces the quality standard.

questions.q: What is the primary purpose of tracking velocity in Scrum?
opts: To measure individual developer productivity and identify who is underperforming
To compare the team's performance against other Scrum teams in the organisation
To set an increasing performance target that motivates the team to work harder each sprint
To help the team make realistic sprint commitments based on what they actually completed in previous sprints
questions.explain: Velocity is a planning tool — it helps the team commit to a realistic amount of work based on their actual throughput in previous sprints.
questions.deep: Using velocity as a performance target or comparison metric is a dangerous anti-pattern. It leads to inflated estimates, over-commitment, incomplete stories, and eventual burnout.

questions.q: A team's average velocity is 25 story points and they commit to 40 for the next sprint. What is the most likely outcome?
opts: The sprint will likely leave stories incomplete
The team will finish early and take more work
The Product Owner will remove stories
The Scrum Master will add a developer
questions.explain: Committing to 40 when averaging 25 almost guarantees incomplete stories. Velocity exists specifically to prevent this kind of over-commitment.
questions.deep: Over-commitment is one of the most common Scrum anti-patterns. It feels ambitious but erodes stakeholder trust when commitments are consistently broken.

questions.q: How does the velocity feedback loop work across sprints?
opts: The Scrum Master sets a new velocity target at the start of each sprint based on team capacity
Each sprint's actual completed points become the baseline for the next sprint's commitment — the loop is self-correcting
The Product Owner sets velocity targets and the team must meet or exceed them each sprint
Velocity is calculated once at the project midpoint and used to forecast the final release date
questions.explain: Each sprint's actual completed points inform the next sprint's commitment. If 28 points were completed, the team plans for approximately 28 next sprint. The system self-corrects.
questions.deep: Over-commit → incomplete stories → lower measured velocity → smaller next commitment → team finishes on time. The feedback loop finds a sustainable rhythm without management intervention.

questions.q: What happens to a team's velocity when they consistently underestimate their sprint workload?
opts: Velocity increases because the team works harder to compensate for the underestimate
The sprint is cancelled and replanned when the team realises they have underestimated
Velocity drops because incomplete stories score zero, then next sprint's commitment decreases until the team finishes on time
Velocity remains stable because story points are re-estimated at the end of each sprint
questions.explain: Underestimating → incomplete stories → lower measured velocity → smaller next sprint commitment → team finishes on time. The feedback loop self-corrects within 2–3 sprints.
questions.deep: The beauty of the velocity feedback loop is that it is self-regulating. Teams don't need a manager to correct their planning — the mechanism does it automatically.

questions.q: What is technical debt in the context of velocity, and why does it matter?
opts: Money owed for software licences and tools
Bugs found during final release testing
The cost of hiring senior developers
Shortcuts that slow future sprint work
questions.explain: Technical debt = shortcuts taken under pressure. Like financial debt, it accrues — future sprints slow down as the team works around messy, untested code.
questions.deep: Paying down technical debt is a legitimate backlog item. Teams that ignore it see velocity gradually decline over time as the codebase becomes increasingly difficult to extend.

questions.q: Why should adding more developers to a team be a last resort when velocity is declining?
opts: New developers need onboarding time and interrupt existing team members, initially reducing velocity before any benefit is seen
New developers are expensive and will always increase the project budget beyond approval limits
New developers change the team's velocity calculation, making historical data unreliable
Adding developers requires renegotiating the contract with the client before proceeding
questions.explain: Brooks' Law: "Adding manpower to a late software project makes it later." New developers need time to onboard and interrupt existing members — initially reducing velocity.
questions.deep: Fred Brooks documented this in "The Mythical Man-Month" (1975). It remains true today. Adding headcount is a last resort, not a first response to declining velocity.

questions.q: A team's velocity is: Sprint 1: 30, Sprint 2: 28, Sprint 3: 18, Sprint 4: 17, Sprint 5: 16. What does this trend most likely indicate?
opts: The Product Owner is consistently adding too many stories to the sprint during planning
The team is accumulating technical debt or has unresolved impediments causing a sustained decline
The team is steadily improving its estimation accuracy over time
This is normal random fluctuation and requires no special action from the Scrum Master
questions.explain: A sustained downward trend — not random variation — signals an underlying problem such as accumulating technical debt, growing codebase complexity, or unresolved team impediments.
questions.deep: This is precisely why the Retrospective exists. Three consecutive sprints of declining velocity demand a serious investigation. The Scrum Master should identify and remove the root cause.

questions.q: A team completes all 10 sprint stories but 3 do not fully meet the Definition of Done. What is their correct velocity?
opts: Zero — the sprint is considered a failure if any story misses the Definition of Done
The full 10 stories' worth of points — the team did complete all the stories
Only the 7 fully done stories' points count — incomplete DoD means zero contribution to velocity
A partial credit based on how close each of the 3 stories came to meeting the DoD
questions.explain: Only stories that fully meet the Definition of Done count toward velocity. Stories 8, 9, and 10 contribute zero points — partial completion is not counted.
questions.deep: This rule prevents "velocity inflation" — claiming high velocity while shipping low-quality work. If partial completion counted, teams would be incentivised to start many stories and finish none properly.

questions.q: Why does a "no overtime" policy help improve long-term velocity rather than reduce it?
opts: Overtime uses budget reserved for later sprints
Overtime is prohibited by law in most workplaces
Agile formally bans overtime in every project
Tired developers create bugs and technical debt
questions.explain: Overtime reduces long-term velocity. Tired developers produce more bugs and technical debt, creating rework that costs far more time than the overtime hours gained.
questions.deep: The Agile principle of "sustainable pace" means maintaining a rhythm the team can sustain indefinitely. Consistent, rested work consistently outperforms irregular crunch cycles over any meaningful time horizon.

questions.q: A manager wants to compare Team A's velocity of 40 points per sprint to Team B's velocity of 25 points per sprint. Why is this comparison problematic?
opts: Story point scales differ between the teams
Teams need identical tools before comparison
Velocity data must never be shared with managers
Teams need identical sprint lengths first
questions.explain: Story points are relative to each team's own calibration. Team A's "5" and Team B's "5" likely represent entirely different amounts of work. Cross-team velocity comparison is meaningless.
questions.deep: Comparing team velocities creates perverse incentives — teams inflate estimates to appear more productive. Velocity is only valid as a within-team planning tool, never as a cross-team benchmark.
